entries.title: Australian GP
entries.headline: Russell Opens 2026 Season With Commanding Melbourne Victory
entries.summary: George Russell delivered a masterclass at Albert Park to claim victory at the 2026 Australian Grand Prix, with Mercedes sending an early statement to the rest of the field.
entries.body:
George Russell led from lights-to-flag in Melbourne to give Mercedes a dominant season opener, controlling the pace throughout and never looking troubled on his way to the chequered flag. A clean start off the front row put him immediately in clear air, and from there the Briton simply managed the gap with the composure that has defined his rise to the top of the sport. Behind him, the battle for the remaining podium positions was fiercely contested, with Lewis Hamilton bringing his Ferrari home in second after a late-race charge pushed him past a fading rival in the closing laps.
The midfield told a more chaotic story, with the new-for-2026 technical regulations producing close and unpredictable racing throughout the pack. Tyre degradation played a bigger role than many teams had anticipated at pre-season testing, catching several strategies out and shuffling the order in the final stint. Lando Norris salvaged a solid points finish for McLaren after a difficult qualifying, while both Audi entries showed genuine race pace that will have rival engineers taking notes. As the paddock heads to Shanghai, Russell leads the drivers' championship and Mercedes sit atop the constructors' standings — exactly the kind of start the Silver Arrows needed after a subdued 2025.

entries.title: Chinese GP
entries.headline: Antonelli Makes History in Shanghai as Mercedes Dominate China Weekend
entries.summary: A weekend to remember in Shanghai — Russell took the sprint, then 19-year-old Kimi Antonelli claimed his maiden Grand Prix win on Sunday to become F1's second youngest ever race winner, with both McLarens failing to even start the race.
entries.body:
Saturday's sprint was a proper scrap. Russell started from pole and immediately had Hamilton breathing down his neck — the Ferrari driver burst forward off the line and actually led in the opening laps before Russell fought back to retake the lead. Behind them Leclerc climbed through to second while Antonelli had a nightmare start, dropped back, got tangled up with Hadjar, and then served a 10-second penalty during the safety car. He still recovered to fifth, which tells you everything about his raw pace. Russell held on to win from Leclerc and Hamilton, keeping his 100% record intact heading into Sunday.
The Grand Prix was where things got really interesting. Antonelli had taken pole in qualifying after Russell hit a gearbox issue, and while Hamilton again got a flying start to briefly lead, Antonelli was back out front before the end of lap two and never gave it up again. Both McLarens — Norris and Piastri — failed to even make the start with electrical issues, handing a massive gift to their championship rivals. Russell recovered from a mid-race scrap with the Ferraris to take second, with Hamilton sealing his first ever podium in Ferrari red in third. At 19 years and 202 days old, Antonelli became just the second teenager in history to win a Grand Prix — only Verstappen was younger. "I'm speechless. I want to cry, to be honest," he said afterwards. Russell meanwhile had his championship lead cut to just four points, and admitted it was going to be a tight year.
